text:
Radical cross-coupling reactions have transformed the synthesis of complex organic molecules, yet these transformations have traditionally remained the exclusive domain of transition metal catalysis or photoredox approaches. While p-block elements have recently been shown to mimic the two-electron redox cycles of late transition metals, their ability to facilitate one-electron pathways typical of first-row metals like nickel or copper has been largely unknown.

This study describes the discovery of a bismuth-mediated radical oxidative addition of redox-active electrophiles. By employing a low-valent bismuthinidene complex, we demonstrate that a main-group element can undergo single-electron transfer (SET) with alkyl phthalimide esters and Katritzky salts. This process allows for the isolation and rigorous characterization of well-defined Bi(III)–C(sp3) intermediates. These adducts exist in equilibrium with their corresponding in-cage radical pairs, exhibiting homolytic bond cleavage and typical radical-type behavior such as Giese additions.

Leveraging this reactivity, we developed a bismuth-catalyzed C(sp3)–N cross-coupling reaction. The system exploits the unique behavior of α-amino alkyl fragments, which undergo bismuth-mediated oxidation to generate electrophilic iminium ions. The transformation operates under mild, autonomously driven conditions—requiring no external photoredox catalysts, chemical oxidants, or electrochemical setups—and enables the functionalization of a variety of NH-heterocycles and bioactive molecules. These findings broaden the scope of pnictogen redox chemistry and establish bismuth radical catalysis as a potent strategy for modern synthetic organic chemistry.
